---
title: "Body Scan Practice"
format: html
---
# Body Scan Practice

A body scan is a mindfulness practice that trains you to notice physical sensations without immediate reaction. In recovery, this helps because urges, stress, and emotions often show up in the body first (tight chest, jaw tension, restlessness, stomach discomfort).

When you detect early body signals, you can intervene sooner with coping skills. Over time, body awareness improves emotional regulation and reduces automatic behavior.

Interoceptive awareness practices like body scanning can improve early detection of stress and craving cues. Earlier detection means earlier intervention and fewer automatic behavior chains.

## Guided Body Scan (8-12 minutes)
1. Sit or lie down comfortably.
2. Bring attention to your breathing for one minute.
3. Slowly scan from feet to head, noticing sensation without judging it.
4. If your mind wanders, gently return to the current body area.
5. End by naming one thing your body needs today.

::: {.callout-note}
### Reflection and action

As you complete this section, link each insight to one specific behavior.

Practice three times this week and note urge/stress before and after.

Small repeatable actions are more useful than perfect one-time efforts.
:::
